inputs.userGoal: Create an Azure IoT Hub edge deployment with AI module for anomaly detection, output events to Cosmos DB, and send SMS alerts via Logic Apps.

reasoning.whyChosen: IoT Edge modules are packaged as containers, and ACR is the native private registry integration for IoT Hub and secure module distribution.
reasoning.tradeoffs: External registries can work but add external dependency and governance complexity compared with first-party ACR.

resources.name: iotHubEdge

reasoning.whyChosen: IoT Hub is the managed control plane for IoT Edge devices and cloud ingestion, supporting secure device identity, module deployment, and message routing.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Event Hubs lacks IoT Edge lifecycle features, while IoT Central is higher-level SaaS with less deployment-level control.

resources.dependencyReasoning: IoT Edge module deployment references container images stored in ACR.

resources.name: edgeDeploymentAnomalyAi

reasoning.whyChosen: Automatic IoT Edge deployment provides centralized rollout of the AI anomaly detection module to tagged edge devices.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Automatic deployments require disciplined device tagging and validation but scale better than manual operations.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Configuration is scoped under IoT Hub and pulls the AI module image from ACR.

references.title: Deploy modules at scale using automatic deployments

reasoning.whyChosen: Cosmos DB provides low-latency JSON storage with global-ready scalability for anomaly events and operational telemetry.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Cosmos DB is typically higher cost at scale than basic storage but provides richer query and throughput behavior for telemetry workloads.

references.title: Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL overview

resources.name: cosmosAnomalyDb

reasoning.whyChosen: A dedicated logical database keeps anomaly workload data isolated and easier to govern.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Shared databases simplify initial setup but increase blast radius and policy coupling.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Database is a child resource of the Cosmos DB account.

references.title: Manage Azure Cosmos DB SQL resources

resources.name: cosmosAnomalyContainer

reasoning.whyChosen: A partitioned container supports efficient write scaling and query filtering by device identity.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Partitioning by device can create skew for highly active devices; anomalyType partitioning can hurt per-device investigation queries.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Container is a child resource under the SQL database.

references.title: Partitioning in Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL

reasoning.whyChosen: Logic Apps provides a low-code orchestrator for alert fanout and SMS integration with minimal operational overhead.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Logic Apps is fast to implement but can be less flexible than custom code for complex branching and testing.

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure Communication Services is the native Azure SMS provider integrated with Logic Apps connectors.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Twilio can have broader international carrier coverage depending on region, while ACS provides tighter Azure-native integration.

overallReasoning.summary: The architecture uses IoT Edge for near-device anomaly inference, IoT Hub for secure control and telemetry ingress, Cosmos DB for anomaly event persistence, and Logic Apps plus ACS for SMS alerting automation.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: This plan prioritizes managed services and speed of delivery over lowest possible cost. Additional hardening (private endpoints, separate environments, and secret rotation automation) can be added in an approved iteration.

plan.validation: All resources are co-located in eastus for simpler operations and lower latency. Dependency ordering ensures module registry and hub are available before edge deployment and that Cosmos logical resources are created in hierarchy order.
